passages.title: The Rise of Community Gardening

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Once considered a niche hobby, community gardening — shared plots of land cultivated collectively by local residents — has seen a dramatic resurgence in cities worldwide over the past two decades, driven by a combination of rising food costs, growing interest in sustainability, and a widely reported desire for stronger neighbourhood connections.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Community gardening in its modern form traces back to the allotment movement in nineteenth-century Britain, when small parcels of land were rented to working-class families to grow their own vegetables, partly to supplement limited wages, and partly out of concern among reformers that urban life was becoming disconnected from nature.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Beyond fresh produce, participants frequently report significant benefits to mental wellbeing. Several studies have found regular gardening activity associated with reduced self-reported stress and anxiety, and some researchers cite exposure to certain soil bacteria as a possible, though not yet firmly established, additional contributing factor to these mood benefits.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Community gardens frequently function as informal social hubs, particularly valuable for isolated elderly residents and newly arrived immigrant families, bringing together people who might not otherwise interact and, in several documented cases, playing a measurable role in gradually reducing local crime rates.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Despite their popularity, community gardens face real logistical hurdles. Waiting lists for a plot commonly extend beyond two years in high-demand areas, land tenure is often insecure, since much community garden land is held only under short-term lease, and disputes between gardeners over plot boundaries or maintenance responsibilities are common enough that most established gardens now have detailed formal rules governing them.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Some city governments have begun formally incorporating community gardens into urban planning policy, rather than treating them merely as a temporary or informal use of vacant land. Seoul, for example, has committed public funding to a significant long-term expansion of its community garden network as part of the city's broader food security strategy.

questionGroups.instructions: The passage has six paragraphs, A–F. Choose the correct heading for paragraphs B–E from the list of headings below.
headingOptions: A network expanding with direct government support
Where the modern movement first began
Bringing together people who would otherwise remain strangers
The practical difficulties of running a shared plot
A possible biological explanation for improved mood
Falling participation among younger generations
How rising food prices changed public attitudes
A ban on gardening in certain cities

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?

questions.statement: Community gardening is a completely new phenomenon with no historical precedent.
Some researchers believe soil bacteria may contribute to the mood benefits of gardening.
Community gardens have been shown in some cases to help reduce local crime rates.
Waiting lists for a community garden plot are rarely longer than six months.
Seoul has decided to stop funding its community garden programme.

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: What historical movement is identified as the origin of modern community gardening?
What kind of land tenure arrangement do many community gardens have?
What do most established community gardens now have, due to frequent disputes?
What broader strategy is Seoul's garden expansion part of?

passages.title: Bioluminescence in the Natural World

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Bioluminescence, the production and emission of light by living organisms, occurs across a remarkably wide range of species, from bacteria and fungi to insects and, most abundantly, marine life. Researchers estimate the vast majority of documented bioluminescent species live in the ocean, particularly at depths where sunlight no longer penetrates.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The light is produced through a chemical reaction involving a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. When luciferin reacts with oxygen in the presence of luciferase, energy is released in the form of light rather than heat, making the reaction remarkably efficient compared with artificial light sources, which waste much of their energy as heat.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: The function of bioluminescence varies significantly between species. Some organisms use it primarily for predation, luring prey with a glowing lure, as in the case of the anglerfish. Others use it defensively, releasing a sudden flash to startle predators or a cloud of luminous material to distract them while the organism escapes, similar in function to an octopus's ink cloud.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Some species use bioluminescence to communicate, most famously fireflies, whose distinctive, species-specific flash patterns allow individuals to identify and attract potential mates of the same species in the dark. Different firefly species have evolved recognisably different flash rhythms, reducing the likelihood of mating with the wrong species.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Though far less common than in marine environments, bioluminescence does occur on land, most notably in certain species of fungi, which produce a faint greenish glow. The evolutionary function of fungal bioluminescence remains genuinely debated among researchers, though one hypothesis suggests the light may attract insects that help disperse the fungus's spores.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Scientists have found practical applications for bioluminescent proteins beyond their natural context. The gene responsible for green fluorescent protein, originally isolated from a bioluminescent jellyfish, is now widely used in laboratories worldwide as a marker to visualise gene expression and protein activity within living cells — a technique that contributed to a Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2008.

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage?

questions.statement: Most documented bioluminescent species are found on land.
Bioluminescence is produced through a chemical reaction involving luciferin and luciferase.
Bioluminescent reactions waste more energy as heat than artificial light sources do.
Firefly flash patterns differ between species.
Scientists fully agree on why bioluminescence evolved in fungi.

questionGroups.instructions: The passage has six paragraphs, A–F. Choose the correct heading for paragraphs B–E from the list of headings below.
headingOptions: A chemical process that wastes little energy as heat
Attracting prey and escaping predators
A discovery that earned international scientific recognition
Species-specific signals used to find a mate
An unresolved question about a land-based glow
The discovery of the first bioluminescent bacteria
Why bioluminescence is rare in freshwater lakes
A ban on collecting bioluminescent jellyfish

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: What two substances react to produce bioluminescent light?
Which animal is given as an example of using a glowing lure to attract prey?
What might fungal bioluminescence help disperse, according to one hypothesis?
In what year did research involving a bioluminescent-derived protein contribute to a Nobel Prize?

passages.title: The Silk Road and the Exchange of Ideas

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: The term “Silk Road” was coined in the nineteenth century by the German geographer Ferdinand von Richthofen, describing a network of trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean. The network was active in various forms for well over a millennium, from roughly 130 BCE, when the Han dynasty formally opened trade with Central Asia, until the fifteenth century CE, when sea routes gradually became more economically important.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Beyond silk, from which the network takes its name, goods moving along the routes included, from the east: paper, gunpowder, porcelain, and tea; from the west: glassware, wool textiles, and gold and silver; and from Central Asia and India: spices, precious stones, and notably horses, highly valued by the Chinese military for their superior breeding compared with domestic Chinese horses.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: The routes facilitated far more than the exchange of goods; they also carried ideas, technologies, and religious beliefs. Buddhism spread from India into China and beyond via merchants and monks travelling the Silk Road. The technology of papermaking, invented in China, reached the Islamic world by the eighth century CE, following the Battle of Talas in 751, and from there reached Europe centuries later.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: The routes were never a single continuous road but rather a shifting network passing through oasis cities such as Samarkand, Bukhara, and Kashgar, which grew wealthy as trading hubs, providing food, water, and lodging for merchant caravans crossing otherwise inhospitable desert and mountain terrain.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Silk Road trade gradually declined for several interconnected reasons. The rise of direct maritime trade routes around Africa, pioneered by European powers from the late fifteenth century, offered a faster and ultimately cheaper alternative. Political fragmentation and periodic conflict along the land routes also made overland trade increasingly unpredictable and risky for merchants.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: The term has since been revived in a modern political and economic context. China's Belt and Road Initiative, launched in 2013, explicitly invokes the historical Silk Road as inspiration for a contemporary programme of infrastructure investment connecting Asia, Africa, and Europe.

questionGroups.instructions: Questions 14 requires TWO answers. Questions 15-17 have one correct answer. Choose the correct letters.

questions.statement: Which TWO of the following goods, according to the passage, travelled from the WEST along the Silk Road?
options: Silk
Glassware
Wool textiles
Spices
Tea

questions.statement: According to the passage, papermaking technology reached the Islamic world following which event?
options: The founding of Samarkand
The Battle of Talas
The launch of the Belt and Road Initiative
The opening of maritime trade routes

questions.statement: Why were horses from Central Asia particularly valued by the Chinese military?
options: They were cheaper than domestic horses
They were considered superior in breeding to domestic horses
They could not be bred in China at all
They were required for religious ceremonies

questions.statement: What does the passage identify as a key factor in the decline of Silk Road trade?
options: A ban on land trade by the Chinese government
The rise of maritime trade routes around Africa
The invention of paper
The founding of Samarkand

questionGroups.instructions: Match each description below with the correct term, A–E.

questions.statement: Coined the term used to describe this network of trade routes.
A religious belief system that spread along the trade routes.
An oasis city that grew wealthy as a trading hub.
An event associated with the spread of papermaking technology.
A modern programme explicitly inspired by the historical trade network.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the summary below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the box for each answer.
questionGroups.summaryText: The Silk Road passed through a network of ___23___ such as Samarkand and Bukhara, which grew wealthy as trading hubs. Buddhism spread partly through the travels of merchants and ___24___. Trade eventually declined due to the rise of ___25___ routes around Africa and political ___26___ along the land routes. In 2013, China launched a modern programme of ___27___ investment inspired by the historical network.

passages.title: Permafrost Thaw and the Global Carbon Cycle

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Permafrost — ground that has remained frozen for at least two consecutive years — underlies approximately a quarter of the exposed land surface in the Northern Hemisphere, storing within it an estimated 1,500 billion tonnes of organic carbon, accumulated over thousands of years from the remains of plants and animals that never fully decomposed in the frozen ground.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: This figure is significant: researchers estimate permafrost currently holds roughly twice as much carbon as is present in the entire atmosphere. As global temperatures rise, permafrost is thawing at an accelerating rate across the Arctic, and previously frozen organic material has begun to decompose, releasing carbon dioxide and methane into the atmosphere.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: This creates what climate scientists term a feedback loop: rising global temperatures cause permafrost to thaw, which releases greenhouse gases, which in turn contributes to further warming and further thaw. Because methane traps significantly more heat than carbon dioxide over a short timeframe, the specific mix of gases released — which depends on whether decomposition occurs in oxygen-rich or oxygen-poor conditions — matters considerably for the pace of this feedback.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Thawing permafrost also poses direct, immediate problems for Arctic communities and infrastructure. Roads, pipelines, and buildings constructed on permafrost can subside or crack as the previously stable frozen ground beneath them shifts, and some Siberian and Alaskan settlements have already been forced to relocate buildings or entire communities as the ground has become unstable.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Much research has historically focused on gradual, top-down permafrost thaw, but scientists have increasingly documented cases of abrupt thaw, in which ground collapses suddenly, sometimes within a matter of months, when ice-rich permafrost melts and the ground surface subsides dramatically, forming distinctive craters and slumping landscapes. Abrupt thaw currently affects a much smaller land area than gradual thaw, but may account for a disproportionately large share of associated carbon release.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Despite growing research attention, considerable uncertainty remains around exactly how much carbon will ultimately be released and over what timescale. Current major climate models are criticised by some permafrost specialists for inadequately representing abrupt thaw processes, meaning that overall projections of future permafrost-related emissions may be systematically too conservative.

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?

questions.statement: Permafrost stores less carbon than is currently present in the atmosphere.
The specific balance of gases released during permafrost thaw affects how quickly the feedback loop accelerates.
Some communities have had to relocate buildings due to thawing permafrost.
Abrupt thaw currently affects a larger land area than gradual thaw.
All current climate models account fully for abrupt permafrost thaw.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the table below. Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.

rows.before: Ground can collapse within a matter of

questionGroups.instructions: Which paragraph contains the following information? Write the correct letter, A–F.

questions.statement: a comparison between the amount of carbon stored in permafrost and in the atmosphere
an explanation of how rising temperatures and gas release reinforce each other
a description of sudden, localised ground collapse
a criticism of how current models handle a particular type of thaw

passages.title: A Guide to Public Library Services

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: MEMBERSHIP TIERS. Standard membership is free for all residents and allows borrowing of up to eight items at a time, for a loan period of three weeks. Premium membership costs twenty-four pounds a year and allows borrowing of up to twenty items, for a loan period of four weeks, plus free reservation of items currently on loan elsewhere. Both membership types include free access to the library's e-book and audiobook collection.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: OPENING HOURS NOTICE. From next month, the Central Library will extend its Saturday opening hours, now open from nine until six, an increase of two hours. Sunday opening hours remain unchanged, from eleven until four. The library will be closed on all public holidays, and will close early, at three o'clock, on Christmas Eve and New Year's Eve.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: UPCOMING EVENTS. Join us for our monthly Book Club, meeting on the second Tuesday of each month at six thirty in the Reading Room — new members always welcome, no booking required. Our Children's Story Time runs every Saturday morning at ten, suitable for ages three to seven. And don't miss our Author Talk with local novelist Grace Fenwick, taking place on the twenty-second — tickets are free but must be booked in advance at the front desk.

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the notices?

questions.statement: Standard membership allows borrowing more items than Premium membership.
Both membership types include access to e-books and audiobooks.
The library's Sunday opening hours are changing.
The library closes early on New Year's Eve.
Tickets for the Book Club meeting must be booked in advance.

questionGroups.instructions: Which notice contains the following information? Write the correct letter, A, B, or C.

questions.statement: a benefit exclusive to one particular type of membership
a change taking effect next month
an event requiring advance booking
an activity suitable for a specific age range

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the notices for each answer.

questions.prompt: How much does Premium membership cost per year?
What time does the Central Library close on Christmas Eve?
On which day of the month does the Book Club meet?
Who is the author giving the talk on the twenty-second?

passages.title: Staff Handbook: Health and Safety at Work

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: All employees are required to complete a health and safety induction on their first day of employment, covering fire evacuation procedures, first aid provisions, and the location of emergency exits. Refresher training must be completed annually thereafter, and records of completed training are kept on file by the HR department for a minimum of five years.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: In the event of a fire alarm, all staff must leave the building immediately via the nearest marked exit, proceeding directly to the designated assembly point in the car park. Lifts must never be used during an evacuation. Staff should not stop to collect personal belongings, and must not re-enter the building until a senior fire warden confirms it is safe to do so.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Any workplace accident or near-miss, regardless of severity, must be reported to a line manager within twenty-four hours and formally recorded in the accident log book kept at reception. This applies even to minor incidents that do not require first aid treatment, since recurring near-misses can indicate an underlying hazard that needs addressing.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Employees working at a display screen, such as a computer monitor, for more than an hour continuously are entitled to a workstation assessment, which can be requested through the HR portal. The assessment covers chair and desk height, monitor position, and lighting, and aims to reduce the risk of repetitive strain injury and eye strain.

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the handbook?

questions.statement: Refresher training only needs to be completed once, at the start of employment.
Training records are kept by HR for at least five years.
Lifts should be used during a fire evacuation if the stairs are busy.
Employees must report even minor accidents that don't require first aid.
A workstation assessment can only be requested by a line manager, not the employee.

questionGroups.instructions: Complete the sentences below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the handbook for each answer.

questions.prompt: New employees must complete an induction covering fire procedures, first aid, and ___.
During an evacuation, staff must go to the assembly point in the ___.
Workplace accidents must be reported to a line manager within ___ hours.
Accidents must be recorded in the accident log book kept at ___.
A workstation assessment can be requested through the ___.

questionGroups.instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

questions.statement: Who must confirm it is safe to re-enter the building after an evacuation?
options: Any member of staff
A senior fire warden
The HR department
The building's owner

questions.statement: Why must even minor near-misses be reported?
options: It is a legal requirement with no other reason given
They can indicate an underlying hazard
They affect insurance premiums
They are used for staff performance reviews

questions.statement: Who is entitled to a workstation assessment?
options: Only senior staff
Anyone using a display screen continuously for more than an hour
Only new employees
Only staff who report discomfort

questions.statement: What does the workstation assessment NOT cover, according to the passage?
options: Chair height
Monitor position
Lighting
Internet speed

passages.title: The History and Revival of Urban Beekeeping

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Keeping honeybees within cities, once a niche or even faintly eccentric pursuit, has grown markedly in popularity across many major cities over the past two decades, with rooftop hives now visible on buildings ranging from budget hotels to prestigious cultural institutions and city halls.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Beekeeping in urban areas is not entirely new. Nineteenth-century Paris had a notable tradition of hives kept within the grounds of the Luxembourg Gardens, some of which remain productive to this day, while London had a small but continuous population of urban beekeepers throughout the twentieth century, even if the practice remained relatively obscure until recently.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Somewhat counterintuitively, researchers have found urban environments can actually support healthy bee populations rather well. Cities typically offer a greater diversity of flowering plants across parks, gardens, and street trees than intensively farmed monoculture rural landscapes, and urban areas are generally several degrees warmer than surrounding countryside, extending the foraging season.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: The recent surge in popularity has, however, raised a genuine concern among some bee specialists. Several cities now host so many hives that local flower resources may be insufficient to adequately support all of the bee colonies present, potentially causing competition not only between managed honeybee colonies but with wild native bee and pollinator species, which some researchers argue deserve greater conservation priority.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: In response to overcrowding concerns, a small number of cities have begun regulating hive density, requiring registration of hives with local authorities, or in some cases capping the total number of hives permitted within a given area — a shift from the largely unregulated approach of a decade ago.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Beyond honey production itself, advocates argue urban beekeeping delivers genuine educational value, connecting city residents, particularly children, with food production and ecology in a direct, hands-on way, and some studies suggest hosting hives can measurably improve pollination of nearby food-growing spaces, including private gardens and community allotments.

questionGroups.instructions: The passage has six paragraphs, A–F. Choose the correct heading for paragraphs B–E from the list of headings below.
headingOptions: A concern that popularity may now be causing harm
Surprising advantages that cities offer bee colonies
A long but little-known urban tradition
New rules introduced in response to a problem
Educational and ecological side benefits
A decline in honey quality over recent decades
Why rural areas are better suited to beekeeping
A ban on hives within city centres

questionGroups.instructions: Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?

questions.statement: Urban beekeeping is a completely new phenomenon with no historical precedent.
Cities can offer bees a longer foraging season than surrounding rural areas.
All bee specialists agree that hive overcrowding is not a genuine concern.
Some cities have introduced caps on the total number of hives permitted.
Urban beekeeping has been shown to have no effect on the pollination of nearby gardens.

questionGroups.instructions: Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: Where in nineteenth-century Paris did a notable beekeeping tradition develop?
What could managed honeybee colonies compete with, according to some researchers?
answer: wild native bee and pollinator species

questions.prompt: What have some cities begun requiring hive owners to do, in response to overcrowding?
Besides private gardens, what other food-growing spaces are mentioned as benefiting from improved pollination?
